startScreenOptions.title: AN35.1-AN35.9 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 4 embedded questions for AN35.1-AN35.9.

interactions.label: A patient develops a retropharyngeal abscess that spreads rapidly into the poste

params.question: A patient develops a retropharyngeal abscess that spreads rapidly into the posterior mediastinum. Which anatomical pathway permits this dangerous spread?

answers.text: The danger space, between the alar and prevertebral fasciae, extends continuously into the posterior mediastinum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The danger space lies between the alar fascia anteriorly and the prevertebral fascia posteriorly. It extends from the skull base to the posterior mediastinum, explaining why retropharyngeal infections can become life-threatening surgical emergencies.

answers.text: The carotid sheath provides a direct conduit from the pharynx to the thorax

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The carotid sheath carries the common carotid artery, internal jugular vein, and vagus nerve — it does not communicate directly with the posterior mediastinum.

answers.text: The investing layer of deep cervical fascia opens inferiorly into the superior mediastinum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The investing layer encircles the neck superficially (enclosing SCM and trapezius) and does not extend into the mediastinum.

answers.text: The pretracheal fascia splits at the thoracic inlet to communicate with the pleural cavity

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The pretracheal fascia merges inferiorly with the fibrous pericardium (anterior mediastinum), not the pleural cavity or posterior mediastinum.

overallFeedback.feedback: The danger space — between the alar and prevertebral fasciae — is the route. It extends from skull base to diaphragm, making retropharyngeal infections potential mediastinal emergencies.
Correct. The danger space is the most clinically important fascial space because it provides an uninterrupted route for infection to spread from the neck into the posterior mediastinum.

metadata.title: A patient develops a retropharyngeal abs

interactions.label: The left subclavian artery has a longer intrathoracic course than the right. Whi

params.question: The left subclavian artery has a longer intrathoracic course than the right. Which anatomical difference explains this?

answers.text: The left arises directly from the aortic arch, whereas the right arises from the brachiocephalic trunk

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The left subclavian artery is the third branch of the aortic arch, giving it a longer intrathoracic segment. The right subclavian arises from the brachiocephalic trunk at the level of the right sternoclavicular joint, so it enters the neck almost immediately.

answers.text: The left passes posterior to the oesophagus before ascending into the neck

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The left subclavian does not pass behind the oesophagus. Its longer course is due to its direct origin from the aortic arch, not a retro-oesophageal path.

answers.text: The left loops around the ligamentum arteriosum before ascending

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: It is the left recurrent laryngeal nerve (from the vagus) that hooks around the aortic arch at the ligamentum arteriosum, not the subclavian artery itself.

answers.text: The left first crosses the midline behind the trachea

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The left subclavian does not cross the midline — it ascends on the left side from the aortic arch. Only the left brachiocephalic vein crosses the midline.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key difference is their origin: the left subclavian arises directly from the aortic arch (longer intrathoracic course), while the right comes from the brachiocephalic trunk (shorter course).
Correct. This asymmetric origin also explains why the left recurrent laryngeal nerve has a much longer course — it loops under the aortic arch rather than the subclavian artery.

interactions.label: In Horner syndrome, the affected pupil is dilated because the sympathetic supply

params.question: In Horner syndrome, the affected pupil is dilated because the sympathetic supply to the dilator pupillae muscle is interrupted.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: The mechanism (sympathetic interruption) is correctly identified, but the effect is wrong. Loss of the dilator pupillae action causes pupillary constriction (miosis), not dilation — the sphincter pupillae acts unopposed.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the statement is false. When sympathetic supply to the dilator pupillae is lost, the parasympathetic sphincter pupillae acts unopposed, causing the pupil to constrict (miosis), not dilate.

interactions.label: A 28-year-old woman presents with wasting of the small muscles of the hand and n

params.question: A 28-year-old woman presents with wasting of the small muscles of the hand and numbness along the medial forearm. Imaging reveals a cervical rib arising from C7. Which structure is most likely compressed?

answers.text: Lower trunk of the brachial plexus (C8, T1)

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A cervical rib compresses the lower trunk of the brachial plexus as it crosses the thoracic outlet. This trunk carries fibres for the intrinsic hand muscles and medial cutaneous sensation, explaining both the wasting and the numbness pattern.

answers.text: Upper trunk of the brachial plexus (C5, C6)

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Upper trunk compression (Erb-Duchenne palsy) produces shoulder abduction and elbow flexion weakness — a 'waiter's tip' posture — not hand muscle wasting.
Ulnar neuropathy at the elbow causes similar hand wasting but does not explain medial forearm numbness (the medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm arises from the medial cord, not the ulnar nerve).
Carpal tunnel syndrome affects the lateral three-and-a-half digits and thenar muscles, not the medial forearm or hypothenar muscles seen in thoracic outlet syndrome.

overallFeedback.feedback: The pattern of medial hand muscle wasting with medial forearm numbness localises to the lower trunk (C8, T1) of the brachial plexus — the structure compressed between a cervical rib and the first rib.
Correct. The neurological form of thoracic outlet syndrome from a cervical rib characteristically compresses the lower trunk (C8, T1), producing medial hand and forearm symptoms.
